emails.subject: The 9% strength gain that didn't translate to cycling power
emails.body_text:
Hello & Welcome
Picture this: Two cyclists finish identical 6-week strength programs.
Cyclist A just squatted 9% more weight than when he started. Impressive, right? Cyclist B never touched a barbell, but his sprint power jumped 6%. Guess which one would drop you in a race?

This actually happened in a landmark 2019 study by Kristoffersen et al., published in Frontiers in Physiology, and it's turned everything we thought we knew about cycling strength training upside down. For decades, the cycling world has been split into two camps:
Camp 1: The Gym Warriors "Heavy squats and deadlifts build the raw power you need for crushing climbs and devastating sprints."
Camp 2: The Bike Purists "Why waste time lifting when you can build cycling-specific strength with big gear intervals?"
Both sides have compelling arguments. Both have produced world champions. But here's what's fascinating: recent research shows they're BOTH right... and BOTH wrong.

The truth is more nuanced than either camp wants to admit, and over the next 7 days, I'm going to show you exactly what the latest peer-reviewed science reveals about:
Why on-bike and off-bike strength training improve endurance through completely different mechanisms
The specific protocols world-class researchers use to maximize strength gains
How to periodize both approaches for year-round improvement

Tomorrow, we'll dive into why the scientific evidence for gym-based strength training is so compelling that even endurance purists can't ignore it. The data is fascinating, and it's going to change how you think about getting stronger on the bike.

Ride strong,
Anthony

P.S. That cyclist who gained 9% in the gym but barely improved his cycling power? By the end of this series, you'll know exactly why this happened and how to avoid the same mistake.

emails.subject: Why endurance cyclists are secretly lifting heavy
emails.body_text:
For years, endurance cyclists avoided heavy weights like the plague.
The fear? Adding bulk. Losing that precious power-to-weight ratio. Becoming slow on the climbs. Then the research started rolling in.

The 17% Breakthrough
Multiple controlled studies found cyclists increased their time-to-exhaustion at high intensity by 17% or more after adding 8-12 weeks of heavy leg training to their programs. That's not sprint power. That's pure endurance. But here's the kicker - these weren't beginners seeing "newbie gains." These were well-trained cyclists already putting out serious watts.

The Science Behind the Magic
A comprehensive 2021 review by Vikmoen & Ronnestad examined concurrent strength+endurance training across multiple studies. The results were clear: both male and female cyclists experience a similar beneficial effect from concurrent training on cycling performance and its physiological determinants compared to normal endurance training only.

When you lift heavy (80%+ of your 1RM), three critical adaptations occur:
1. Improved neuromuscular efficiency - you recruit more muscle fibers with less effort
2. Enhanced cycling economy - you use less oxygen at the same power output
3. Increased fractional utilization of VO2max - you can sustain a higher percentage of your max longer

The Endurance Gains Protocol:
Gym Protocol (Based on Ronnestad et al. studies):
2 sessions per week
3-4 sets of 4-10 reps at 85-90% 1RM
Focus on: Half squats, leg press, step-ups, single-leg hip flexion
3-4 minutes rest between sets
8-12 week blocks

The "Anti-Bulk" Truth
The fear of adding muscle mass is completely overblown. A 2016 study by Vikmoen et al. showed that strength training improved cycling performance, fractional utilization of VO2max and cycling economy in female cyclists while increasing muscle cross-sectional area. The cyclists got faster, not slower.

Real-World Results
Ronnestad's research consistently shows:
Improved mean power output in a 5-min all-out trial following 185 min of submaximal cycling
Reduced oxygen consumption and heart rate during prolonged efforts
Better power output at lactate threshold

Why This Matters for You
If you're still avoiding the gym because you're worried about "getting bulky" or "losing your endurance," you're leaving massive performance gains on the table. The science is clear: properly programmed strength training makes you faster, not slower.

Tomorrow, I'll show you the flip side - how cyclists are achieving similar strength gains without ever setting foot in a gym, using protocols that some argue are even more cycling-specific. Plus, I'll give you your first complete workout protocol you can use immediately.

The plot thickens...
Ride strong,
Anthony

emails.subject: The workout that rivals heavy squats
emails.body_text:
What if I told you that researchers have found a way to build strength that rivals gym training - without ever leaving your bike?
A groundbreaking 2024 study published in Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise by Barranco-Gil et al. did exactly that.

The 2024 Game-Changer
For the first time, researchers directly compared on-bike vs off-bike strength training in well-trained cyclists. The results shocked the cycling world:
Both the off-bike and on-bike groups showed similar improvements in overall and pedal-specific muscle strength, power, and muscle mass over 10 weeks.
Even more interesting: the on-bike group showed unique adaptations - gains in tendon thickness that the gym group didn't achieve.

Why On-Bike Strength Works
According to the research, high-torque cycling efforts create three unique adaptations:
Movement specificity - You're training the exact muscle firing patterns used in cycling
Tendon adaptation - Tendons become stiffer and more efficient at power transfer
Neuromuscular coordination - Your nervous system learns to produce force in the exact context it needs to

The On-Bike Strength Workout (Based on Barranco-Gil et al., 2024)
WARM-UP (15 minutes): 10 min easy spinning (RPM: 85-95, Zone 1-2) then 5 min progressive increases
MAIN SET - High-Torque Strength Training:
All-out efforts against very high resistance at very low cadences
From near-standstill in biggest available gear
10-15 second maximum efforts (will start at ~40-50 RPM)
4-6 efforts with 3-5 minutes complete recovery
Key: Focus on maximum force production, not speed
COOL DOWN (10 minutes): 10 min easy spinning (RPM: 85-95, Zone 1)

The Safety Advantage
Here's something crucial the 2024 study revealed: the gym group reported more injury-related symptoms over the 10-week program. The on-bike group? Zero injuries.
For masters athletes especially, the injury risk-to-benefit ratio often favors on-bike strength work.

But Here's The Problem...
While this single workout is powerful, it's just one piece of a much larger puzzle.
The researchers used a complete 10-week periodized program that systematically built strength, converted it to cycling power, and then maintained it through racing season. They had detailed progressions, recovery protocols, and knew exactly when to emphasize gym vs bike work.

Most cyclists try to piece this together themselves. They don't build on each other, no clear progression or periodization, conflicting gym and bike stress at the wrong times, zero idea how to maintain gains during racing season.

That's Exactly Why I Created My Complete Strength & Conditioning Plan
I've taken every research study I'm sharing this week and turned them into a complete, year-round system.
Get The Complete Research-Based S&C Plan Here

You get:
- The exact 12-week periodized progression
- Exact Exercises
- Video demonstrations of every exercise
- How to maintain gains during race season

Tomorrow, I'll reveal which method produces superior sprint power - and why the answer isn't what you'd expect.
Ride strong,
Anthony

emails.subject: +6% vs +1% - The method that builds explosive power
emails.body_text:
1 second. That's how long it takes to separate the sprinters from the also-rans in a bunch finish.
And according to groundbreaking research published in Frontiers in Physiology, your choice of strength training could mean the difference between winning and watching from the back.

The Kristoffersen Experiment
In 2019, Norwegian researchers put 28 well-trained cyclists through two different 6-week strength programs:
Group 1: Heavy gym training (half squats, leg press, etc.)
Group 2: 6-second all-out bike sprints
The results weren't even close.
The bike sprint group boosted their peak sprint power by 4-6%. The gym group? A measly 1%.
Even more telling: when tested in a fatigued state (after prolonged cycling), the sprint-trained group's power advantage widened to +6% vs +2%.

Why Specificity Rules in Sprinting
The research backs this up. While the gym group got 9% stronger in the half squat, this raw strength didn't transfer to cycling power in the short term.

The Sprint Protocol (Based on Kristoffersen et al., 2019)
WARM-UP (15 minutes): 10 min easy spinning (Zone 1-2) then 3 x 10-second progressive sprints (50%, 70%, 90%) with 2 min recovery
MAIN SET - Maximum Sprint Intervals:
6-second all-out sprints from standstill start, seated position
Resistance individually adjusted to achieve highest possible power output at 110-120 RPM
Complete recovery: 5-7 minutes easy spinning between efforts
Three sets of four intervals with 2 min active recovery between intervals, and 5 min active recovery between sets
2-3 sessions per week
COOL DOWN (10 minutes): 10 min easy Zone 1 spinning

Real-World Transfer
Here's the crucial part: the sprint training group significantly improved their 55-meter sprint performance on their own bikes by 4.3%, while the gym group saw no improvement in actual cycling sprint performance.
The lab gains transferred perfectly to real-world cycling.

But What About Long-Term Development?
Here's where it gets interesting. While on-bike training wins for immediate sprint gains, the gym group built a foundation of raw strength that could pay dividends over longer training periods.
Elite track cyclists have known this for decades - they use gym work in the off-season to build a strength base, then convert it to cycling-specific power as competitions approach.

Here's What Most Cyclists Get Wrong...
They try to do both gym and bike strength work simultaneously without understanding:
Which phase of training to emphasize each method
How to avoid interference between strength and endurance adaptations
When to convert gym strength into cycling-specific power
How to maintain strength gains during high-volume training blocks
The research studies had detailed periodization plans that avoided these conflicts.

That's why I created my Complete Strength & Conditioning Plan - to give you the exact same systematic approach the researchers used.
Get The Complete Research-Based S&C Plan Here

Tomorrow, I'll reveal the hybrid approach that combines the best of both worlds for maximum results.
Ride strong,
Anthony

emails.subject: How lifting weights makes you climb faster
emails.body_text:
It sounds completely counterintuitive.
How can spending time lifting heavy objects in a gym make you better at riding up mountains for hours? Yet the research is overwhelming: cyclists who add strength training to their endurance programs consistently outperform those who don't.
The question isn't whether it works anymore. It's HOW it works.

The VO2max Mystery
A fascinating 2024 study by Hebisz et al., published in PLoS One, compared two groups of well-trained cyclists on identical 8-week training programs. The only difference? One group did all their intervals at normal cadence (80+ RPM), while the other did them at low cadence (50-70 RPM).
The low cadence group showed significantly greater improvements in VO2max, maximal aerobic power, VT1, VT2, and minute pulmonary ventilation. The freely chosen cadence group only improved VO2max (in absolute volume).
Think about that: same training load, same time commitment, dramatically different adaptations just by changing cadence.

The Dual Pathway Theory
Research suggests strength training improves climbing through two separate mechanisms:
Pathway 1: Peripheral Adaptations (Muscle Level)
Improves tendon stiffness for better power transfer
Increases capillarization in working muscles
Result: Higher sustainable power output

The Economy Effect
Vikmoen et al. showed strength training improved cycling economy in female cyclists - essentially, you use less energy to maintain the same pace.
Studies consistently show economy improvements of 7-8% after 12 weeks of strength training. At threshold power, that translates to riding 15-20 watts faster for the same perceived effort.

The High-Torque Endurance Protocol
Based on the Hebisz study, here's how to use low-cadence work to boost your aerobic capacity:
MAIN SET - Aerobic Torque Intervals:
High-intensity intervals (4-6 repetitions, 4 minutes each at 90-100% maximal aerobic power) performed at low cadence 50-70 RPM
Gear: Large chainring, maintain steady torque
Recovery: 5 min easy spinning (85+ RPM) between intervals
Critical: Resist urge to increase cadence when it gets hard

The Combination Effect
Here's where it gets really interesting: combining gym and on-bike torque work creates a synergistic effect that neither method achieves alone.
The gym builds raw force production capacity. The on-bike work converts that force into cycling-specific power. Together, they create climbing performances that surprise even experienced riders.

But the challenge is knowing EXACTLY how to combine them...
Most riders who've read the research know they should be doing both. But they don't have a systematic plan that tells them:
EXACTLY when to do gym
HOW to progress through each phase
WHAT to do when life gets in the way
HOW to integrate with their existing training
WHEN to back off to avoid overreaching

So they end up with random strength sessions that conflict with their cycling training and produce mediocre results.
The researchers in every study I've shared had detailed, systematic plans that optimized everything.

That's exactly what you get in my Complete Strength & Conditioning Plan.
Get The Complete Research-Based S&C Plan Here

Tomorrow, I'll give you your complete action plan to get started immediately - whether you choose the hybrid approach or focus on just one method.
Ride strong,
Anthony

emails.subject: How to get 15% stronger in 12 weeks (the periodized approach)
emails.body_text:
What if I told you there was a way to get the sprint power benefits of on-bike training AND the raw strength gains of gym work?
That you could have your cake and eat it too? Research has figured this out, and scientists are using a periodized approach that's producing some of the most significant strength gains ever recorded in cycling studies.

The 15% Solution
Multiple studies show cyclists can increase their leg strength by 15% or more in 12-16 weeks using a combined approach. But here's the crucial part: they don't do everything at once. They periodize it.

The Periodization Breakthrough
Think of strength development like building a house:
Foundation Phase: Raw strength from gym work
Construction Phase: Converting strength to cycling-specific power
Finishing Phase: Race-specific applications

Research consistently shows this approach works because:
Progressive overload: Each phase builds on the previous
Specificity increase: Training becomes more cycling-specific as races approach
Recovery optimization: Different stress types at different times prevent overtraining

Your 12-Week Hybrid Plan (Based on Combined Research)

PHASE 1: Foundation (Off-Season, 4-6 weeks)
Focus: Building raw strength capacity
Gym Sessions (2x/week):
Half squats: 3 x 4-10 RM
Single-leg leg press: 3 x 4-10 RM
Single-leg hip flexion: 3 x 4-10 RM
3-4 minutes rest between sets
Progressive overload week to week

PHASE 2: Conversion (Pre-Season, 6-8 weeks)
Focus: Converting gym strength to cycling power
Gym Sessions (1-2x/week):
Maintain strength: 3 x 6-8 @ 70-75% 1RM
On-bike (2-3x/week):
High-torque sprint intervals (all-out efforts against very high resistance)
Aerobic torque intervals at 50-70 RPM
Traditional sprint intervals for specificity

PHASE 3: Race Application (In-Season)
Focus: Race-specific power applications
Gym Sessions (1x/week):
Maintenance only
On-bike:
Sprint intervals for peak power (6-second all-out efforts)
Race-specific efforts
Torque maintenance intervals

Real-World Results
Cyclists following periodized approaches typically see:
10-15% increase in maximum strength (squat/leg press)
4-6% improvement in sprint power
Improved cycling economy and fractional utilization of VO2max
Better performance in all-out efforts following prolonged cycling

The Individual Factor
Research shows both male and female cyclists respond similarly to concurrent training. The key variables are:
Your current strength level (weaker riders see bigger initial gains)
Training history (new to gym = faster adaptations)
Recovery capacity (affected by age, sleep, stress, nutrition)
Season timing (foundation work in off-season, maintenance in-season)

This is where having a complete, systematic plan becomes critical. The researchers in every study I've shared had detailed protocols and progressions that made the difference.
Get The Complete Research-Based S&C Plan Here

Tomorrow, I'll give you your complete action plan to get started immediately.
Ride strong,
Anthony

emails.subject: Start here: Your first 4 weeks to cycling strength
emails.body_text:
Seven days ago, you might have been confused about whether to hit the gym or stay on the bike for strength training.
Today, you have access to knowledge based on peer-reviewed research that most cyclists never see. You understand:
Why specificity rules for sprint power development (6% vs 1% improvements)
How both gym and bike training improve endurance through different mechanisms
The periodized approach that maximizes both methods
The exact protocols used in published research studies

Now it's time to act.

Your 4-Week Quick-Start Plan
Whether you're new to strength training or looking to optimize what you're already doing, here's your immediate action plan:

WEEK 1-2: Assessment Phase
Choose Your Path:
Option A: Gym Focus (Based on Ronnestad protocols)
Test your current strength levels safely
Start with 3 x 8-10 @ 60-70% estimated 1RM
Focus on: Half squats, single-leg leg press, hip flexion exercises
Add: 1x/week low-cadence intervals for specificity

Option B: On-Bike Focus (Based on research protocols)
Start with high-torque efforts: 6-8 x 10-15 seconds from near-standstill
Add low-cadence intervals: 4 x 4 minutes at threshold power, 50-70 RPM
Focus on form: Maximum force production, smooth pedal stroke

WEEK 3-4: Progressive Overload
Based on your chosen path, increase either:
Gym loads by 5-10% (if form remains perfect)
Torque interval duration by 2-3 minutes
Sprint interval volume by 1-2 efforts per session

The Critical Success Factors
After analyzing hundreds of research studies, three factors determine success:
Consistency: Better to do less consistently than more sporadically
Progressive overload: Gradually increase difficulty over time
Recovery: Strength training stress requires adequate recovery to avoid injury

Why Most Cyclists Fail at Strength Training
It's not lack of motivation or knowledge. It's lack of a systematic, periodized approach that integrates with their cycling training. They try random gym workouts or throw in some big gear efforts without understanding how it fits into their bigger picture.
The researchers in every study had detailed protocols and progressions that made the difference.

Your Complete Solution
Everything I've shared this week represents the cutting edge of cycling strength research:
6% sprint power improvements (Kristoffersen study)
17% improvements in time-to-exhaustion (multiple studies)
Similar strength gains from gym vs bike training (Barranco-Gil study)
Significant VO2max improvements from low-cadence training (Hebisz study)

The Bottom Line
You now have more knowledge about cycling strength training than 95% of cyclists ever will. You understand the peer-reviewed science, you know the proven protocols, you have the action plan.
But knowledge without a systematic plan to implement it is just entertainment.
The researchers didn't just discover what works - they had detailed progressions, timing, and periodization that made it work.

Get The Complete Research-Based S&C Plan Here

Your Next 30 Minutes
Here's what I want you to do right now:
Decide your approach: Do you want to piece this together yourself or get the complete research-based system?
Take action: Either start with the basic protocols I've shared or get the complete plan
Schedule your first session: Block time in your calendar this week

The research is clear (Kristoffersen et al., 2019). The methods are proven (Barranco-Gil et al., 2024). The only variable left is your decision to act.

Your faster, stronger cycling self is waiting. The only question is how quickly you want to get there.

Get The Complete Research-Based S&C Plan Here
Ride strong,
Anthony

P.S. Remember cyclist A from Day 1 who gained 9% gym strength but barely improved his cycling power? He made one critical mistake: he didn't have a systematic plan to convert his gym gains to cycling performance. Don't make the same mistake.
